author: Naval
content: Drones are easy to make, hard to stop, and bring Mutually Assured Destruction to conventional warfare.

author: Naval
content:

“Does this mean that traditional software engineering is dead? Absolutely not. Software engineers—even the ones who are not necessarily tuning or training AI models—these are now among the most leveraged people on earth. Sure, the guys https://t.co/Aw8Uk8Bj0b

author: Naval
content: Pure software is rapidly becoming un-investable.

author: Naval
content:
AI is turning the Internet into the Library of Babel.

https://t.co/iQRCdJ5mXm

author: Naval
content:
Vibe Coding Is the New Product Management

“There’s been a shift—a marked pronouncement in the last year and especially in the last few months—most pronounced by Claude Code, which is a specific model that has a coding engine in it, which is so good that I think now you have vibe https://t.co/6H2u6zWnu2

author: Naval
content: If you do not direct your attention, it will be directed for you.

author: Naval
content:
A few geniuses solve problems and automate solutions for the rest of society.

Any society that can overcome envy to maximize the number and output of geniuses will thrive.

author: Naval
content:
The cost of code is coming down, so we will consume more of it.

The productivity of coders is going up, so they will become more valuable.

Coding now includes training and driving models.

author: Naval
content: If you stop moving for long enough, you will be forced to confront the essential emptiness of life.

author: Naval
content: Careers are dead. Jobs are dying. Opportunities arising.

author: Paul Graham
content: Amjad showed me Replit's latest stuff. They're about to redefine vibe coding in a way that will seem obvious in retrospect. A lot of the biggest ideas have that quality.

author: Paul Graham
content: One thing we do in office hours is cook up bold long-term plans to tell investors about to convince them that the startup has at least some chance of being huge. But often as not these then become the startup's actual plan.

author: Paul Graham
content: We had two minutes left at the end of one startup's office hour slot, so we took a shot at finding a new name for them, and we actually found one. That is definitely a record.

author: Paul Graham
content: In office hours with one startup today we came up with a plan that would continue to work even if the model companies had eaten all the other markets. That's a first.

author: Paul Graham
content:
I just reread "How to Do Great Work." It's so long! But it also has less fat than most things I've written, which is a weird combination, because usually writing that's long on the macro scale is long on the micro scale too.

https://t.co/Bs2gq7bSkJ

author: Paul Graham
content: Visited Clicky Bezel in SF. It was very interesting to see the work of multiple independent watchmakers all in one place. And they actually had a timegrapher in the store! Want to know how accurate some model is? Just walk over and measure it.

author: Paul Graham
content: Incidentally, how many CEOs would have the confidence to do an AMA about this topic at this moment?

author: Paul Graham
content: In retrospect I was seeing the future, but I didn't fully grasp it at the time, because it just seemed so weird.

author: Paul Graham
content: At breakfast there was a 2 year old standing up on a plastic chair that seemed about to tip over, and I found I was automatically calculating the best path to him and how long it would take to get there. Once you have kids it never turns off.

author: Paul Graham
content: If you're working on an early stage startup, don't be deterred from using Anthropic just because you might want to sell to the DoD one day. Early on you need to focus on making your product the best. If you get the best results from Anthropic models, use them.

author: Aaron Levie
content:
Almost everything I've learned about startups:

* Aim to get to cash flow positive early
* Don't overly optimize for private market valuations
* Build a great team and culture
* Make sure the business model *works*
* Focus, focus, focus. Dilution of effort will crush you

author: Alex Hormozi
content:
Had a convo with a young founder who was “stuck” this morning. 

Here was my parting advice:

1) Change directions less. 
2) Stick with things long enough to see if it really worked. And if you don’t know yet. Then keep doing it. 
3) The more stable you are the more stable your

author: Andreas Klinger 🦾
content:
So many disucssion how, when, what to do when you start a company…

- 80h/30h?
- VC/bootstrapped?
- Local/remote?

Here the only startup advice i ever found to be true:

1) Do whatever you think is right.
2) If you end up successful, you were right (for your path)

🤷‍♀️

author: Sahil Lavingia
content:
The benefit of building in public is an audience when you launch.

The cost is the public embarrassment if you fail.

The trick is realizing no one cares that you failed. They’re impressed that you tried.

More importantly, you have a massive bug tester squad so you reach stability as a platform much faster.
